worth_it_explanation: Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals carry negotiated terms that the open market does not, so they reward a publisher with higher prices and committed demand when they run as agreed. A deal that has stalled, lost its buyer, or sits below an open-market floor that outbids it returns no value while still occupying the negotiation that created it. This check reads the deal configuration and recent spend a publisher already has in Microsoft Monetize, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a specific deal a publisher can revive, retire, or reprice.
intro: Microsoft Monetize carries PMP and PG deals alongside the open auction, each with a deal ID, a buyer, negotiated pricing, and a priority that decides where the deal sits against open-market demand. This subcategory is about the health and pricing of those deals, whether they are still active and spending and whether their floors and priorities let them transact as intended. These checks confirm that the deals a publisher negotiated are doing the work they were set up to do rather than sitting stalled or being undercut by the open market.

audit_checks.title: Deal health is not monitored for active spend or stalled deals
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A negotiated Private Marketplace or Programmatic Guaranteed deal that stops spending returns nothing while the buyer relationship and the inventory commitment remain in place. Without a regular read of deal-level spend and status, a stalled deal, an expired deal ID, or a buyer who walked away goes unnoticed, so the publisher keeps inventory reserved against demand that is no longer arriving.

educate.base:
A Private Marketplace (PMP) deal and a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal each represent a negotiated arrangement between a publisher and a buyer, carried through Microsoft Monetize as a deal ID with its own pricing and targeting. A PMP deal invites a named buyer into an auction at a negotiated floor, and a PG deal commits a fixed volume of impressions to a buyer at a fixed price. Both depend on the deal continuing to transact, and both can stop transacting without any error appearing in the console.

Deals stall for ordinary reasons. A buyer pauses or ends a campaign on their side, a deal ID expires at the end of its negotiated term, a targeting change on either side stops the deal from matching available impressions, or the buyer shifts spend to a different deal. When that happens, the deal stays in the system as a configured arrangement while its spend drops to zero, so the inventory it was meant to fill either goes to the open market or goes unsold.

Monitoring deal health means reading deal-level spend and status on a regular cadence rather than at negotiation time only. The goal is to separate three states for each active deal: spending as expected, spending below the committed or expected level, and not spending at all. A deal in the second or third state is a signal to contact the buyer, check the deal ID and its term, or retire the deal so it stops reserving attention.

A few signs point to deal health going unmonitored:
  • No regular report breaks out spend, impressions, and win rate by deal ID
  • Deals with a zero or near-zero spend over a recent window sit alongside active deals with no follow-up recorded
  • The publisher cannot say which deals are pacing to their committed volume and which have stalled
  • Expired or paused deal IDs remain in the active deal list with no review date
You may want to confirm that deal-level spend and status are reviewed on a set cadence. One approach is to build a deal-level report covering a recent window, flag any deal spending below its expected level, and follow up with the buyer or retire the deal.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, sponsorship-adjacent and seasonal campaigns often run as PG deals with a committed volume, so a stalled deal can leave a premium placement unfilled against a commitment that was supposed to cover it. You may want to track PG deals against their committed pacing specifically, so a deal that falls behind its volume is caught while there is still term left to recover it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, PMP deals with retail and brand buyers can concentrate on product and category inventory that carries higher value. You may want to monitor those deals by the inventory they target, so a deal that stops spending on the commercial pages is caught rather than masked by open-market fill stepping in at a lower price.

investigate.intro: Review the active deal list and a recent deal-level spend report to see which deals are transacting, which are spending below expectation, and which have stalled or expired.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the deals section and list the active Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals with their deal IDs, buyers, pricing, and term dates.
For each deal, note its status, whether the deal ID is within its negotiated term, and what inventory and targeting it covers.
Record which deals are PG with a committed volume and which are PMP, given that the expectation for spend differs between them.
Ask the publisher whether deal-level spend and status are reviewed on a regular cadence, and record when the last review happened.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal ID over a recent window, with revenue, impressions, win rate, and for PG deals the delivered volume against the committed volume.
Flag any deal with zero or near-zero spend over the window, and any PG deal pacing behind its committed volume.
Compare each deal's recent spend against its earlier spend to separate deals that are spending as expected from deals that have slowed or stopped.
Note whether the data shows healthy deals only or whether stalled and underpacing deals exist without follow-up on record.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per deal ID, its status, term dates, recent spend, and for PG deals its delivered volume against the committed volume, alongside whether deal health is reviewed on a regular cadence. A setup where deals are monitored on a set cadence and stalled or underpacing deals are followed up or retired passes. A setup where deals carry zero or near-zero spend, expired deal IDs sit in the active list, or no regular review exists is a fail recorded with the specific deals affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's status, recent spend, and PG delivered-versus-committed volume above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor deal health on a regular cadence, so stalled and underpacing Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals in Microsoft Monetize go unnoticed. A negotiated deal that stops spending returns nothing while it still reserves inventory and attention, and a PG deal that falls behind its committed volume can miss the commitment before anyone follows up. {{voice_recommend}} building a deal-level report over a recent window that breaks out spend, win rate, and PG delivered-versus-committed volume, flagging any deal spending below expectation, and either reviving the deal with the buyer or retiring it. After that, set a regular cadence to read deal health so a stalled deal is caught while there is still term to recover it.

audit_checks.title: Deal floors and priorities are misaligned with the open market
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal exists to transact at its negotiated terms, but if the open-market floor sits above the deal price or the deal priority does not let it win against equal open-market demand, the deal loses the impressions it was negotiated to serve. The reverse also costs the publisher. A deal priced or prioritized to win impressions the open market would have cleared higher trades open-market revenue for deal volume.

educate.base:
A deal price and the open-market floor are two prices that compete for the same impression, and a publisher decides how they relate through the deal's negotiated floor and its priority in Microsoft Monetize. The aim is to let a deal win the impressions it was negotiated to serve without letting it win impressions the open market would have cleared at a higher price. Getting that relationship wrong costs the publisher in one of two directions.

In the first direction, the deal is undercut. If a unified open-market floor sits above the deal's negotiated price, or the deal's priority does not let it win against an equal open-market bid, the deal cannot transact even when the buyer is bidding. The impression goes to the open market instead, the deal under-delivers, and a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) commitment can miss its volume through no fault of the buyer. The publisher negotiated demand it is then unable to serve.

In the second direction, the deal undercuts the open market. If a deal is given a priority or a price advantage that lets it win impressions the open auction would have cleared higher, the publisher fills the deal at the expense of revenue it would otherwise have collected. This matters most for PMP deals that share inventory with strong open-market demand. A deal floor set below the open-market clearing price hands those impressions to the deal buyer at a discount.

Microsoft Monetize, which is Prebid-compatible, follows the same deal mechanics Prebid documents, where deals can be set to win over equal open-market bids and where the deal floor and line-item priority decide the outcome. The audit question is whether each deal's floor and priority let it transact as negotiated while still protecting open-market revenue on the inventory it shares.

A few signs point to deal floors and priorities being misaligned:
  • A deal under-delivers while the inventory it targets shows healthy open-market fill, which suggests the open-market floor or a priority gap is blocking the deal
  • A unified or open-market floor sits above the negotiated price of a deal meant to win that inventory
  • A deal wins inventory whose open-market clearing price is consistently higher than the deal price, which suggests the deal is priced or prioritized below the market
  • Deal priorities were set once at launch and never reviewed against how the deals and the open market actually clear
You may want to confirm that each deal's floor and priority match its negotiated intent. One approach is to compare each deal's price and priority against the open-market floor and clearing price on the same inventory, then adjust whichever side is out of line.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a PG deal on a premium placement needs a priority that lets it win against the open market so it can meet its committed volume, while a PMP deal sharing strong inventory needs a floor that does not undercut what the open market would pay. You may want to check premium placements specifically, where a misaligned floor or priority moves the most revenue.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals often target the higher-value product and category inventory, where open-market demand can be strong. You may want to confirm that PMP deal floors on those pages sit at or above the open-market clearing price, so a brand deal does not win commercial impressions at a discount the open auction would have beaten.

investigate.intro: Review each deal's negotiated floor and priority against the open-market floor and clearing price on the same inventory, and look for deals that under-deliver or that win impressions the open market would have cleared higher.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the deals section and record each Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal's negotiated floor, its priority, and the inventory it targets.
Open the pricing or floor configuration for that same inventory and record the open-market floor that applies to it.
For each deal, compare its negotiated price against the open-market floor on the shared inventory and note any case where the floor sits above the deal price.
Record whether deal priorities were set deliberately against the open market or set once at launch and left unreviewed.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize reporting
steps: Build a report that pairs deal-level delivery, with impressions and win rate by deal ID, against open-market fill and average clearing price on the same inventory.
Flag any deal that under-delivers while its target inventory shows healthy open-market fill, which points to a floor or priority blocking the deal.
Flag any deal that wins inventory whose open-market clearing price is consistently higher than the deal price, which points to a deal priced or prioritized below the market.
Note whether the data shows deal floors and priorities aligned with the open market or diverging in either direction.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per deal, its negotiated floor, its priority, and the open-market floor and clearing price on the same inventory, alongside the deal's delivery against expectation. Deals whose floor and priority let them transact as negotiated without winning inventory the open market would have cleared higher pass. A deal undercut by an open-market floor above its price or by a priority gap, or a deal winning impressions below the open-market clearing price, is a fail recorded with the deal and the inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's floor and priority alongside the open-market floor and clearing price on the same inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} carries deal floors and priorities that are misaligned with the open market in Microsoft Monetize, so deals either lose impressions they were negotiated to serve or win impressions the open market would have cleared higher. A unified floor above a deal price or a priority gap blocks the deal and can leave a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) commitment short, while a deal priced below the open-market clearing price fills at the expense of revenue the auction would have collected. {{voice_recommend}} comparing each deal's negotiated floor and priority against the open-market floor and clearing price on the same inventory, then adjusting whichever side is out of line so deals transact as negotiated while open-market revenue holds. After the change, recheck deal delivery and open-market fill on the shared inventory to confirm the two are working together rather than against each other.
